notes: Encoded PowerShell with external indicator; clear staged execution. Related evt-0002 confirms remote download.
Mimikatz on a domain controller is unambiguous credential theft. Critical priority.
Office process spawning PowerShell that fetches a remote payload is a textbook macro-delivery chain.
Scheduled task pointing to a user-writable path is a persistence mechanism. Medium because it is on a dev laptop, not prod.
Rundll32 loading from user temp is a known process-injection pattern. Same host (hr-laptop-04, mchen) had a macro-spawned PowerShell earlier in the day.
Three failed SSH logins from a single external IP within 10 seconds is brute force. Severity is medium, not high, because no successful login followed.

AD enumeration by an administrator account is ambiguous: could be legitimate audit work or recon. Same account ran mimikatz at 11:33:45, one minute earlier — that context elevates suspicion. Investigate the session.
Remote WMI execution by a service account is unusual but may be legitimate audit tooling. Resolves with: confirm whether svc_audit's documented job includes remote whoami enumeration.

Service install from C:\ProgramData at 02:14 local time on finance03 is unusual. Same host and user (jsmith) ran encoded PowerShell at 08:14 the same day — temporal proximity raises suspicion. Investigate the service binary.
curl|bash from an external IP is a textbook ingress tool transfer. The only ambiguity is whether agarcia ran it intentionally — but the pattern itself is high-risk regardless.
Administrator running a named maintenance script during business hours from a controlled path. ExecutionPolicy bypass alone is too low-signal to alert on.
Opening a macro-enabled document is a precondition, not an attack. Without observed macro execution or child processes, this is benign noise.
apt-get upgrade by root at noon is scheduled patching. Alerting on this is rule misconfiguration.
Service account login during expected operating hours. Documented backup operation.
PowerShell bypass loading a stager script at 23:55 from a host already implicated in earlier malicious activity (jsmith on finance03).
